prs.title: feat(feat): Add production-grade SmilesTokenizer with batching and PyTorch support
prs.body:
## Summary
This PR introduces a reusable `SmilesTokenizer` utility for DeepChem to support sequence-based molecular models. 

The tokenizer provides a unified interface for converting SMILES strings into token sequences or numerical IDs, supporting character-level, atom-aware, and BPE (Byte Pair Encoding) subword tokenization.

## Features
- **Flexible Tokenization**: Supports `char`, `atom`, and `bpe` levels.
- **Chemically Aware**: `atom` mode preserves bracketed atoms, stereochemistry, and charges using an optimized regex.
- **Efficient Batching**: `batch_encode` for high-throughput processing.
- **DeepChem & PyTorch Integration**: `tokenize_dataset` utility for direct conversion to padded PyTorch tensors.
- **Persistence**: `save_vocab` and `load_vocab` for model deployments.

## SMILES Syntax Handling
The tokenizer correctly handles complex SMILES constructs including:
- bracketed atoms `[Cl]`, `[NH3+]`, `[C@H]`
- stereochemistry indicators `@`, `@@`
- bond types `=`, `#`
- ring closures `1-9`, `%10`

## Example Usage
```python
from deepchem.feat import SmilesTokenizer
tokenizer = SmilesTokenizer(level="atom")
tokenizer.train(["CCO", "C[Cl]"])
tokens = tokenizer.encode("C[Cl]")
# ['<BOS>', 'C', '[Cl]', '<EOS>']
```

## Testing
Comprehensive unit tests verify:
- Atom-level boundaries for complex chemistry.
- Batching accuracy.
- Persistence round-trip.
- PyTorch tensor generation.

Closes #4777

prs.title: feat(watch): show file-level error summary in watch mode

labels.name: For Uncommitted Bug
labels.description: PR for untriaged, rejected, closed or missing bug

prs.body:
This change updates the watch mode error reporting to include a detailed summary of errors per file, similar to non-watch mode.
It uses 'getErrorSummaryText' to generate the summary and introduces a new diagnostic message '_0_Watching_for_file_changes' to display it alongside the watch status.

Verification:
- Verified manually with reproduction case (single error and zero errors).
- Ran existing 'tscWatch' tests and updated baselines for 213 scenarios.
- All 454 watch mode tests passed.

prs.title: feat: Persistent per-tool style settings
prs.body:
# Excalidraw Per-Tool Settings (#10666) - Report & PR Prep

## Phase 2 — Root Cause Analysis

### Current Behavior
In Excalidraw, style properties (color, stroke width, fill, etc.) are stored as top-level properties in the `AppState` (e.g., `currentItemStrokeColor`). When an element is drawn, it inherits these values. When an element is selected, the global `AppState` is updated to match that element's styles.

### Root Cause
Styles propagate across tools because there is only **one shared state** for each property in `AppState`. The switching logic for tools (`setActiveTool`) focuses on the visual active state and cursor but does not transition the styling configuration.

### Design Intent
The current architecture prioritizes simplicity and a "global brush" metaphor. However, as Excalidraw evolves towards more complex sketching (High-frequency pen use), this global state becomes a source of friction for users who frequently switch between a "Pencil" (for sketching) and "Shapes" (for diagramming).

---

## Phase 3 — Design Decision

### Choice: Option B (Hybrid Per-Tool Styles)
Each tool remembers its last-used style. When switching tools, the "Brush" is swapped for the tool's specific preset. If no preset exists (first use), it inherits the current global style.

**Justification:**
- **Low Cognitive Load**: Users don't need to manually save presets.
- **Natural UX**: Mimics "Picking up a different pen."
- **Backward Compatible**: Doesn't change the data structure of elements.

---

## Phase 7 — PR Description

### Table of Contents
- [Problem](#problem)
- [Solution](#solution)
- [Design Decisions](#design-decisions)
- [How to test](#how-to-test)

### Problem
Changing styles in one tool (e.g., set Pencil to Blue) affects all other tools (Rectangle becomes Blue). This forces users to constantly re-adjust styles when switching between different types of work (e.g., sketching vs. annotation).

### Solution
Implemented a tool-scoped style cache within `AppState`.
- New property: `currentItemStyles: Record<ToolType, Partial<AppStateStyleProperties>>`.
- Automatic sync: Global styles are persisted to the active tool's cache on every change and tool switch.
- Automatic restoration: Preferred styles are restored when switching "to" a drawing tool.

### Design Decisions
1. **Drawing Tools Only**: Persistence is skipped for "Selection", "Hand", and "Eraser" to keep the experience predictable.
2. **Real-time Sync**: We sync both on property change and tool switch to ensure state isn't lost on page reloads.
3. **Implicit Presets**: No new UI buttons are added. The tool "just remembers" what you last did with it.

### How to test
1. Draw a **Red Rectangle**.
2. Switch to **Pencil**, change color to **Blue**.
3. Switch back to **Rectangle**. It should be **Red**.
4. Change **Rectangle** to **Bold**.
5. Switch to **Arrow**. It should have its own last-used width (or default), not necessarily Bold.
6. Verify settings persist after page refresh.

Closes #10666

prs.title: feat: Implement Pixel Eraser for FreeDraw elements
prs.body:
### Description
Implement "Pixel Eraser" (partial erasure) for FreeDraw elements.

Fixes #10741

### Problem
Users could not partially erase sketches or handwritten notes. The eraser tool only supported deleting entire objects, which is frustrating for drawing workflows.

### Solution
Implemented a vector-based partial eraser using geometric stroke splitting.
- **Geometry Logic**: Calculates intersections between the eraser "capsule" and FreeDraw segments.
- **Behavior**: 
    - When the eraser touches a **FreeDraw** element, it effectively "subtracts" the eraser path from the stroke, splitting it into fragments.
    - For **Standard Shapes** (Rectangles, Arrows), the eraser retains the existing "Delete Object" behavior.
- **Performance**: Operations are performed on the specific hit element's points, keeping performance high even with many objects.

### Technical Details
- Added `pixelEraser.ts` for intersection math.
- Updated `EraserTrail` to distinguish between object-erase candidates and pixel-erase candidates.
- Updated `App.tsx` to handle real-time mutation of FreeDraw elements during the eraser drag event.

### Verification
1. Draw a line with the Pencil tool.
2. Select Eraser.
3. Drag through the middle of the line -> The line splits.
4. Drag off the end of the line -> The line shortens.
5. Verify Undo/Redo works as expected.

prs.title: fix(interaction): enable drawing shapes over text elements (#10735)
prs.body:
## What does this PR do?
Fixes an issue where drawing tools (Arrow, Line, Rectangle, etc.) were blocked when starting a pointer down event on top of a Text element. This PR allows these tools to ignore the Text element during hit-testing, enabling users to draw over text (e.g., for annotations).

## Related Issue
Closes #10735

## Changes Made
- Modified `hitElement` logic in `App.tsx`.
- Added a check to return `false` (ignore collision) if:
    - The active tool is a drawing tool (arrow, line, freedraw, rectangle, diamond, ellipse).
    - The element being hit is a Text element.
- Initial binding logic is preserved (arrows can still bind to text), but the text element itself won't capture the event to start editing/selection during drawing.

## Verification
- Verified manually that arrows and shapes can be drawn starting from inside a text element's bounding box.
- Verified that text selection and editing still work when using the Selection tool.

prs.title: Fix: <add more LeetCode 50 solutions and comments for explanation >
prs.body:
This pull request adds complete MySQL solutions for all 50 LeetCode SQL problems, each written in a clean, readable format with detailed inline explanations and comments. A new folder named /leetcode_SQL_50_solutions_mysql/ has been created, containing 50 .sql files. Each file is named according to the problem number and title (for example, 175_combine_two_tables.sql) for easy navigation.

Every file includes:

The problem number and title (e.g., (175) Combine Two Tables)

A MySQL-compatible query solution

Detailed comments explaining the logic, use of joins, filtering, aggregation, and reasoning behind the approach

The following LeetCode problems are covered in this PR:
Combine Two Tables (175), Second Highest Salary (176), Nth Highest Salary (177), Rank Scores (178), Consecutive Numbers (180), Employees Earning More Than Manager (181), Duplicate Emails (182), Customers Who Never Order (183), Department Highest Salary (184), Department Top Three Salaries (185), Delete Duplicate Emails (196), Rising Temperature (197), Trips and Users (262), Game Play Analysis I–IV (511, 512, 534, 550), Managers with at least 5 Direct Reports (570), Employee Bonus (577), Find Customer Referee (584), Investments in 2016 (585), Customer Placing Most Orders (586), Big Countries (595), Classes More Than 5 Students (596), Sales Person (607), Tree Node (608), Not Boring Movies (620), Exchange Seats (626), Swap Salary (627), Customers Who Bought All Products (1045), Actors and Directors Who Cooperated (1050), Product Sales Analysis I–III (1068, 1069, 1070), Project Employees I–II (1075, 1076), Sales Analysis I–III (1082, 1083, 1084), User Activity for the Past 30 Days I–II (1141, 1142), Article Views I–II (1148, 1149), Market Analysis I (1158), Capital Gain/Loss (1393), Top Travellers (1407), Group Sold Products by Date (1484), Find Users With Valid Emails (1517), Customers Who Visited but Not Purchased (1581), and Percentage of Users Attended (1633).

Each query has been tested and verified on MySQL 8+, ensuring accuracy and compatibility. The codebase is formatted for readability, making it easy for beginners to learn from.

This contribution enhances the repository by providing a complete educational resource for SQL learners and competitive programmers
